title: Artificial Intelligence as National Power
subtitle: Implications of the 2025 U.S. National Security Strategy
title_normalized: artificial intelligence as national power: implications of the 2025 u.s. national security strategy

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief analyzes the treatment of artificial intelligence in the 2025 U.S. National Security Strategy. It argues that AI is repositioned as a foundational state capability and a cross-domain force multiplier embedded in military power, economic security, technological sovereignty, alliance management, and standards-setting. The brief interprets the strategy as a move from universal AI governance toward capability-driven, sovereign, and alliance-centered AI state capacity.
abstract: This policy brief examines the implications of the 2025 U.S. National Security Strategy for global AI governance and national power. It argues that the NSS treats artificial intelligence not as an emerging technology but as a foundational state capability linked to military effectiveness, economic resilience, technological sovereignty, alliance alignment, and standards-setting. The analysis identifies a shift from AI regulation toward AI state capacity, interprets the document's limited attention to AI risk governance as strategic silence, and concludes that global AI governance is moving into a fragmented, security-driven, post-breakthrough phase in which political and institutional risks increasingly supersede purely technical risks.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on AI-Enabled Warfare, Sustainability, and Global Security Governance
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on AI-enabled warfare, defense sustainability, AI governance, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.framework_name: AI as National Power / AI-State Capacity Framework
methodology.source_basis: Analytical reading of the 2025 U.S. National Security Strategy using public information and policy-interpretive analysis
methodology.case_context: 2025 U.S. National Security Strategy and its implications for AI governance, technology sovereignty, and national power
methodology.core_problem: AI is increasingly treated as a foundational state capability and geopolitical alignment instrument rather than as a standalone innovation or ethics-regulation issue.
central_claims: AI is positioned in the 2025 NSS as a foundational state capability rather than an emerging technology sector.
AI governance is reframed from ethical regulation toward control of access, supply chains, standards, and technological sovereignty.
U.S. AI strategy is alliance-centric, using technology ecosystems, infrastructure, and standards to bind partners and exclude rivals.
Global AI governance is fragmenting as national security exceptions become structural rather than temporary.
Primary AI risks increasingly shift from purely technical failure toward political and institutional concentration, governance asymmetry, and lock-in.

analytical_lenses.name: AI–State Complex
analytical_lenses.description: A framework in which AI systems shape state capacity, economic competitiveness becomes inseparable from security readiness, and technological leadership functions as deterrence.

analytical_lenses.name: Strategic Silence on AI Risk Governance
analytical_lenses.description: The brief interprets the NSS's limited discussion of transparency, explainability, and systemic AI risk as a signal that AI risks are managed internally within national security frameworks rather than negotiated as universal global norms.

analytical_lenses.name: Standards as Structural Power
analytical_lenses.description: AI standards are treated as instruments shaping interoperability, platform dependence, and long-term institutional lock-in.

analytical_lenses.name: Post-Breakthrough AI Development
analytical_lenses.description: The NSS is interpreted as assuming AI capability as already strategically present, shifting attention from technical feasibility to control, capture of benefits, and constrained diffusion.

dimensions.name: Military Power
dimensions.description: AI functions as a cross-domain force multiplier supporting autonomy, decision-speed advantage, and cost-efficient defense systems.

dimensions.name: Economic Security
dimensions.description: AI is tied to productivity growth, reindustrialization, supply-chain resilience, and domestic capture of technological benefits.

dimensions.name: Technological Sovereignty
dimensions.description: AI leadership is linked to intellectual property protection, standards-setting dominance, and control over technology diffusion.

dimensions.name: Alliance-Centric Governance
dimensions.description: AI cooperation, advanced technology sharing, and access to U.S. AI ecosystems are treated as tools of diplomacy and strategic alignment.

dimensions.name: Standards Power
dimensions.description: Standards shape interoperability, platform dependence, and long-term institutional lock-in.

dimensions.name: Political and Institutional Risk
dimensions.description: Dominant risks shift from model failure and algorithmic bias toward institutional concentration, governance asymmetry, and political lock-in.

key_findings: AI is treated as a foundational state capability, not as an emerging technology.
AI governance is reframed from ethical regulation to control of access, supply chains, and standards.
U.S. AI strategy is alliance-centric, using technology and standards to bind partners and exclude rivals.
Global AI governance is fragmenting, with national security exceptions becoming structural rather than temporary.
Primary AI risks are increasingly political and institutional, not purely technical.
The 2025 NSS embeds AI across military power, economic security, and technological sovereignty rather than treating it as a sectoral innovation issue.
AI standards operate as instruments of long-term structural power by shaping interoperability, platform dependence, and institutional lock-in.
The NSS signals a post-breakthrough phase in which AI capability is assumed and strategic focus shifts to control, domestic benefit capture, and international diffusion constraints.

policy_implications.title: Treat AI governance as state-capacity governance
policy_implications.description: AI policy should be analyzed as a component of national power, institutional capacity, industrial strategy, and alliance architecture rather than as a narrow technology-regulation issue.

policy_implications.title: Account for structural fragmentation in global AI governance
policy_implications.description: Universal AI governance regimes are likely to face persistent pressure from export controls, security exceptions, and alliance-based technology blocs.

policy_implications.title: Analyze AI standards as geopolitical infrastructure
policy_implications.description: Standards-setting should be evaluated as a source of interoperability control, platform dependence, and institutional lock-in.

policy_implications.title: Shift risk analysis toward political and institutional risks
policy_implications.description: AI risk assessment should include institutional concentration, governance asymmetry, sovereign capability competition, and political lock-in alongside technical safety concerns.
